title: Salad Shirazi
subtitle: Persian cucumber, tomato, and onion salad with dried mint
excerpt: A finely diced Persian salad of cucumber, tomato, and red onion dressed with lemon juice, olive oil, and dried mint. Deceptively simple and relentlessly refreshing — the key is cutting everything uniformly small, salting the cucumber to remove excess water, and letting the salad sit for a few minutes before serving so the vegetables absorb the dressing.
sourceNotes: Salad Shirazi takes its name from Shiraz in southern Iran. It is documented in Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986) and Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) as a standard accompaniment to Persian rice dishes and kababs. The dried mint is the ingredient that distinguishes it from a simple tomato and cucumber salad.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dress up to 30 minutes before serving. Do not make more than 1 hour ahead as the cucumber continues to release water.

content.text: The cutting is what makes salad Shirazi. Every component should be diced to approximately the same small size — about 5 mm cubes. This ensures each forkful has all three components in equal proportion and gives the salad a satisfying uniform texture. Large, rough-cut vegetables produce a completely different result. Use a sharp knife; a food processor makes the pieces uneven and bruises the tomato.

Dried mint is not a substitute for fresh mint — it has a more concentrated, slightly dusty flavour that is specific to this salad. Fresh mint is a different flavour profile. Use good dried mint or crush dried mint between your fingers before adding to release the volatile oils. The salad needs 10 minutes of resting time after dressing for the flavours to combine.

items.prepNote: peeled, deseeded, and cut into 5 mm dice
deseeded and cut into 5 mm dice

content.text: Salt the diced {{cucumber}} and leave in a colander for 10 minutes to drain excess water. Pat dry. Combine the cucumber, {{tomato}}, and {{red-onion}} in a bowl. Whisk together the {{lemon}} juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, {{mint}}, and salt. Pour over the vegetables and toss gently. Taste and adjust lemon and salt. Leave for 10 minutes before serving to allow the flavours to meld.

items.cause: Cucumber not salted and drained, or tomatoes not deseeded
items.fix: Salt and drain the cucumber for 10 to 15 minutes. Deseed the tomatoes by cutting in half and squeezing out the seeds and gel before dicing

items.symptom: Red onion flavour is too aggressive
items.fix: Soak the diced red onion in cold water for 10 minutes, then drain. This removes much of the harsh pungency while retaining the flavour

items.cause: Dried mint not fresh enough or insufficient lemon
items.fix: Good dried mint should be fragrant when crushed. If yours is old and dusty, replace it. Adjust lemon juice until the dressing is bright and sharp
Fold the vegetables gently once or twice in the dressing — no vigorous tossing. The pieces are small enough that a gentle fold distributes the dressing evenly

content.text: Salad Shirazi appears on every Iranian table as the refreshing counterpart to rich stews and rice. It performs the same function as a Greek salad in Greek cooking — cutting through fat, adding brightness, providing the fresh element that the main dishes lack. The salad takes its name from Shiraz, a city in southern Iran known for its gardens, its poetry (Hafez and Saadi both came from Shiraz), and its sophisticated food culture. In Iran it is often eaten as a side at the same time as the main dish, spooned alongside the rice rather than as a separate course.
